text: The laparoscopic view shows ICG fluorescence diffused around the rectum, visible through infrared detection.
The surgeon mobilizes the left-sided colon and performs high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, using laparoscopic instruments to manipulate and secure the vascular structures.
The laparoscopic view reveals the hypogastric nerve, visible within the surgical field during the rectal cancer procedure.

The surgeon makes a skin incision around the tightly closed anus to initiate the transperineal approach. A gel point mini device is connected, and three ports, including a camera port, are utilized for the procedure.

The video displays the operative field orientation relative to the anus in the lower left corner. The surgeon divides the fat tissues in the ischioanal fossa, revealing the underlying levator ani muscle.

The surgeon begins posterior dissection, dividing the levator ani muscle just above the coccyx from the posterior side towards the bilateral sides. The steep curve of the dissection plane is visible, requiring careful navigation to avoid entering the mesorectum.
Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence is visible, highlighting the appropriate plane of dissection amidst the exudate fluid caused by preoperative radiotherapy.
The surgeon elevates the posterior side of the rectum, revealing fibrous connective tissues covering the mesorectum, which are then carefully divided using electric cautery.
The surgeon continues the posterior dissection towards the sacral promontory, maintaining careful navigation through the anatomical structures.

The surgeon carefully avoids dissecting behind the hypogastric nerve, which is visually lifted upward. The posterior dissection is connected with the established dissection layer using a conventional laparoscopic approach.

The surgeon extends the dissection to the lateral side, identifying the pelvic splanchnic nerves at the five o'clock position on the left side. These nerves serve as important landmarks during the lateral dissection, and care is taken to avoid injuring them.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection behind the transverse perineal muscle, carefully separating tissue layers on the anterior side of the rectum.
The surgeon uses electric stimulation to distinguish between striated and smooth muscle, with the striated muscle visibly responding to the electric cautery while the smooth muscle does not.
On the right anterior side, the surgeon uses electric cautery to divide the levator ani muscle fibers, revealing the ICG-stained endopelvic fascia beneath.
The green fluorescence of ICG highlights the endopelvic fascia, serving as a landmark. The cut end of the levator ani muscle is visible.
The endopelvic fascia is visibly stained green with ICG. After dividing this stained fascia, the surgeon identifies the neurovascular bundle beneath.

The surgeon identifies the arms of the puborectalis sling at the one o'clock position and divides the puborectalis muscle using electric cautery. Dissection near the neurovascular bundle results in a small amount of bleeding.
The surgeon observes the endopelvic fascia, which is stained green with ICG, highlighting its location within the surgical field.
The surgeon cuts the smooth muscle, adjusting the dissection line in response to visible bleeding points.

The surgeon encounters increased fibrosis in the dissection area due to preoperative chemoradiotherapy, making it difficult to identify a clear dissection layer. The smooth muscle is visible, and special care is taken to avoid injuring the urethra during dissection.

The surgeon identifies the neurovascular bundle as a key landmark for establishing the appropriate dissection line. The proper dissection plane is visualized just behind the prostate, facilitating continued dissection along this plane. Anterior dissection is extended laterally while preserving the neurovascular bundle, progressing until reaching the pouch of Douglas.

The surgeon extends the dissection bilaterally between the mesorectum and neurovascular bundle. The anterior view from the transperineal approach reveals the perineal body, neurovascular bundle, and the cut end of the levator ani muscle.

The surgical team transitions to the abdominal approach, with the left, anterior, and right sides of the surgical field identified. The rectal mobilization is completed, and the resected specimen is extracted through the anus.
